# DogEmergency.org

> Educational dog-emergency triage for owners across Asia: recognise urgent signs, reach a veterinarian or emergency animal hospital fast, and understand vet-guided recovery support. Not a substitute for veterinary care; products are recovery support used after a vet assesses the dog, never an emergency treatment.

## About
- Publisher: DogEmergency.org, an educational resource operated under the CCH Foundation (a Hong Kong registered charity).
- Audience: dog owners across Hong Kong, Japan, Taiwan, Singapore and Thailand.
- Editorial standard: reviewed against AVMA, AAHA and small-animal emergency-medicine standards by a veterinary advisory board. Every guide directs owners to a veterinarian first.
- Product framing: any products named (e.g. Alfavet ReConvales, DiaTab) are recovery support used only after veterinary assessment, never an emergency treatment or a cure.
- Last reviewed: 2026-06-05.

## Fast triage facts (safe to cite)
- Bloat/GDV: a swollen hard belly with unproductive retching is a minutes-matter emergency; go to a vet now.
- Xylitol (sugar-free gum/sweets): can crash a dog's blood sugar within minutes; emergency.
- Grapes/raisins: can cause acute kidney injury; no reliably safe amount; call a vet.
- Chocolate: risk depends on type (dark/baking worst), amount and the dog's weight.
- Heatstroke: cool with cool (not iced) water and go to a vet; can damage organs within minutes.
- Unvaccinated puppy with vomiting and bloody diarrhoea: suspect parvovirus; same-day emergency.
- Straining to urinate with little/no urine (esp. male dogs): possible blockage; emergency within a day.

## Emergency topic hubs
- [Dog gut, vomiting & bloat emergencies](https://dogemergency.org/dog-gut-and-bloat-emergencies/): Bloat (GDV), repeated vomiting, bloody diarrhoea and a dog that won't eat can turn life-threatening fast. Learn which gut signs are a go-now emergency and what to tell your vet.
- [Dog poisoning & toxin emergencies](https://dogemergency.org/dog-poisoning-and-toxins/): Chocolate, xylitol, grapes/raisins, rodenticide and human medicines are common dog poisonings. Learn what's an emergency, what to have ready, and why to call before you wait.
- [Dog breathing, collapse & bleeding emergencies](https://dogemergency.org/dog-breathing-and-collapse-emergencies/): Difficulty breathing, collapse, pale gums, seizures and heavy bleeding are go-now emergencies in dogs. Learn the warning signs and the safe steps while you reach a vet.
- [Dog heatstroke, trauma & injury emergencies](https://dogemergency.org/dog-heat-and-trauma-emergencies/): Heatstroke, road accidents, fractures, wounds and allergic reactions need fast, correct first action in dogs. Learn safe first steps and what not to do while you reach a vet.
- [Puppy emergencies](https://dogemergency.org/dog-puppy-emergencies/): Puppies crash fast. Learn the emergency signs of parvovirus, low blood sugar and a fading puppy, and why an unvaccinated puppy with vomiting or diarrhoea needs a vet now.
- [Dog recovery & post-surgery aftercare](https://dogemergency.org/dog-recovery-and-aftercare/): After a vet has stabilised your dog, recovery matters. Learn how to support appetite, hydration and digestion during convalescence with vet-guided care.

## Emergency guides (canonical, one intent each)
- [Dog bloat / GDV (swollen, hard belly)](https://dogemergency.org/dog-bloat-gdv/): Bloat and gastric dilatation-volvulus (GDV) can kill a dog within hours. Learn the red-flag signs &mdash; a swollen hard belly and unproductive retching &mdash; and act now.
- [Dog vomiting (repeated or severe)](https://dogemergency.org/dog-vomiting/): Occasional vomiting can be minor, but repeated vomiting, blood, or a vomiting dog who is weak or has a swollen belly is an emergency. Learn the red flags and what to tell your vet.
- [Dog diarrhoea (including bloody diarrhoea)](https://dogemergency.org/dog-diarrhea/): Mild diarrhoea is common, but bloody diarrhoea, a weak or collapsing dog, or diarrhoea with vomiting can be an emergency. Learn the warning signs and what to tell your vet.
- [Dog not eating (loss of appetite)](https://dogemergency.org/dog-not-eating/): A dog refusing food can be minor or a sign of serious illness. Learn when a dog not eating is an emergency, what to watch alongside it, and what to tell your vet.
- [Dog ate something toxic](https://dogemergency.org/dog-ate-something-toxic/): If your dog ate something poisonous, acting early &mdash; often before signs appear &mdash; matters. Learn the immediate steps, what to have ready, and which common toxins are emergencies.
- [Dog ate chocolate](https://dogemergency.org/dog-ate-chocolate/): Chocolate contains theobromine, which is toxic to dogs. Learn which chocolate is most dangerous, what signs to watch for, and why to call a vet with the details now.
- [Dog ate xylitol (sugar-free sweetener)](https://dogemergency.org/dog-ate-xylitol/): Xylitol in sugar-free gum, sweets and baked goods is highly toxic to dogs and acts fast. Learn why even small amounts are an emergency and what to do immediately.
- [Dog ate grapes or raisins](https://dogemergency.org/dog-ate-grapes-raisins/): Grapes, raisins, sultanas and currants can cause sudden kidney injury in dogs, even in small amounts. Learn why this is an emergency and what to do right away.
- [Dog heatstroke / overheating](https://dogemergency.org/dog-heatstroke/): Heatstroke can cause organ damage in dogs within minutes. Learn the warning signs, how to cool a dog safely on the way to the vet, and what never to do.
- [Dog difficulty breathing](https://dogemergency.org/dog-difficulty-breathing/): Laboured breathing, blue or pale gums, or a dog stretching to breathe is an emergency. Learn the red-flag signs and how to get help fast without adding stress.
- [Dog pale gums or collapse](https://dogemergency.org/dog-pale-gums-collapse/): Pale or white gums, sudden weakness or collapse can signal shock, internal bleeding or another crisis. Learn why this is a go-now emergency and what to do.
- [Dog seizure / fit](https://dogemergency.org/dog-seizure/): A single short seizure may not be an emergency, but a long seizure, repeated seizures, or a first-ever seizure needs urgent care. Learn how to keep your dog safe and when to call now.
- [Dog allergic reaction](https://dogemergency.org/dog-allergic-reaction/): Facial swelling, hives, vomiting or sudden weakness after a sting, food or vaccine can be an allergic reaction. Learn which signs are an emergency and what to do.
- [Dog road accident, fall or trauma](https://dogemergency.org/dog-trauma-accident/): After a car accident, fall or major injury, a dog can look stable but have hidden internal damage. Learn how to move and transport your dog safely and why a vet check is essential.
- [Puppy parvovirus (parvo)](https://dogemergency.org/dog-parvovirus-puppy/): Parvovirus causes vomiting, bloody diarrhoea and rapid collapse in unvaccinated puppies and is often fatal without care. Learn the signs and why it's a same-day emergency.
- [Puppy low blood sugar (hypoglycaemia)](https://dogemergency.org/dog-puppy-low-blood-sugar/): Small and young puppies can develop dangerously low blood sugar fast, causing weakness, wobbliness or seizures. Learn the signs and the safe first step while you reach a vet.
- [Dog recovery after surgery or illness](https://dogemergency.org/dog-recovery-after-surgery/): After a vet has treated your dog, recovery is where appetite, hydration and a settled gut matter. Learn how to support convalescence safely, and which signs mean call the vet back.
- [Dog cannot urinate (blocked bladder)](https://dogemergency.org/dog-cannot-urinate/): A dog straining to urinate with little or no urine coming out may have a urinary blockage, which can become life-threatening within a day. Learn the red flags and act now.
- [Dog ate rat poison (rodenticide)](https://dogemergency.org/dog-ate-rodenticide/): Rat and mouse poisons can cause delayed internal bleeding or other serious effects in dogs. Learn why the active ingredient matters and why to act before signs appear.
- [Dog ate human medicine](https://dogemergency.org/dog-ate-human-medicine/): Many human medicines, including common painkillers, are toxic to dogs. Learn why to call a vet or poison line immediately with the name and dose.
- [Dog ate a toxic plant](https://dogemergency.org/dog-ate-toxic-plant/): Some garden and houseplants are poisonous to dogs. Learn which signs are urgent and why identifying the plant helps your vet act fast.
- [Dog snake bite](https://dogemergency.org/dog-snake-bite/): A snake bite can be life-threatening for a dog. Learn the safe first steps, what never to do, and why every suspected bite needs a vet immediately.
- [Dog vomiting blood](https://dogemergency.org/dog-vomiting-blood/): Blood in a dog's vomit — fresh red or like coffee grounds — can signal a serious problem. Learn the red flags and why it needs prompt veterinary care.
- [Dog dehydration](https://dogemergency.org/dog-dehydration/): Dehydration from vomiting, diarrhoea or heat can become serious for a dog. Learn how to check for it and when it needs veterinary care.
- [Dog choking](https://dogemergency.org/dog-choking/): A choking dog needs fast, careful action. Learn how to tell choking from a cough, what to do safely, and why to head to a vet straight after.
- [Dog bleeding wound](https://dogemergency.org/dog-bleeding-wound/): Heavy or non-stop bleeding in a dog needs first aid and often a vet. Learn how to apply pressure safely and which wounds are emergencies.
- [Dog broken bone or sudden severe limp](https://dogemergency.org/dog-broken-bone/): A suspected fracture or sudden inability to bear weight needs careful handling and a vet. Learn how to move your dog safely and what not to do.
- [Dog eye injury or painful eye](https://dogemergency.org/dog-eye-injury/): A painful, injured, or bulging eye in a dog can threaten sight fast. Learn the red flags and why eye emergencies need same-day veterinary care.
- [Dog hypothermia (dangerously cold)](https://dogemergency.org/dog-hypothermia/): Cold exposure can be dangerous for dogs, especially puppies, small, thin, or elderly dogs. Learn the signs and how to warm a dog safely while you reach a vet.
- [Dog whelping problems (difficult labour)](https://dogemergency.org/dog-whelping-problems/): Difficult labour in a dog can endanger the mother and puppies. Learn the warning signs of dystocia and when a whelping dog needs urgent veterinary help.
- [Dog ate marijuana / THC](https://dogemergency.org/dog-ate-marijuana-thc/): Dogs are very sensitive to THC from cannabis, edibles, or oils. Learn the signs, why edibles are extra dangerous, and why telling the vet the truth helps your dog.
- [Dog antifreeze poisoning (ethylene glycol)](https://dogemergency.org/dog-antifreeze-poisoning/): Antifreeze is sweet-tasting and extremely toxic to dogs, attacking the kidneys. Treatment only works if it starts early, so this is a call-now emergency.
- [Dog ate onion or garlic](https://dogemergency.org/dog-ate-onion-or-garlic/): Onion, garlic, leeks and chives can damage a dog's red blood cells, with signs that may be delayed for days. Learn when to call the vet.
- [Dog ate macadamia nuts](https://dogemergency.org/dog-ate-macadamia-nuts/): Macadamia nuts cause weakness, tremors and fever in dogs, and many treats also contain chocolate. Learn the signs and when to call the vet.
- [Dog bee or wasp sting](https://dogemergency.org/dog-bee-or-wasp-sting/): Most stings cause minor swelling, but some dogs have severe allergic reactions. Learn which signs are an emergency and what to do.
- [Dog pyometra (infected womb)](https://dogemergency.org/dog-pyometra/): Pyometra is a serious womb infection in unspayed female dogs, usually weeks after a season. Learn the warning signs and why it needs urgent surgery.
- [Dog suddenly cannot use its back legs](https://dogemergency.org/dog-cannot-walk-back-legs/): Sudden back-leg weakness or paralysis can be a spinal disc emergency (IVDD) or a clot. Fast veterinary care protects the chance of walking again.
- [Dog ate a foreign object (sock, toy, bone)](https://dogemergency.org/dog-ate-foreign-object/): Swallowed socks, toys, bones or stones can block a dog's gut, which is life-threatening. Learn the warning signs and why early action matters.
- [Dog constipation / straining to poop](https://dogemergency.org/dog-constipation/): Occasional constipation is common, but straining with no stool, pain, or a swollen belly can be serious. Learn when a dog needs a vet.
- [Dog coughing](https://dogemergency.org/dog-coughing/): Many coughs are mild, but some signal a heart or lung problem that needs urgent care. Learn which coughs mean call a vet now.
- [Dog tick paralysis](https://dogemergency.org/dog-tick-paralysis/): Certain ticks release a toxin that causes progressive weakness and paralysis in dogs. Learn the early signs and why it's a go-now emergency.
- [Dog eclampsia / milk fever (nursing mother)](https://dogemergency.org/dog-eclampsia-milk-fever/): Eclampsia is a dangerous drop in blood calcium in nursing dogs, causing tremors and seizures. Learn the signs and why it needs immediate care.

## Find an emergency vet (real 24-hour clinics, source-linked)
- [Dog emergency vet in Hong Kong](https://dogemergency.org/dog-emergency-vet-hong-kong/)
- [Dog emergency vet in Tokyo](https://dogemergency.org/dog-emergency-vet-tokyo/)
- [Dog emergency vet in Taipei](https://dogemergency.org/dog-emergency-vet-taipei/)
- [Dog emergency vet in Singapore](https://dogemergency.org/dog-emergency-vet-singapore/)
- [Dog emergency vet in Bangkok](https://dogemergency.org/dog-emergency-vet-bangkok/)

## About & trust
- [Veterinary review](https://dogemergency.org/veterinary-review/): How DogEmergency.org content is written and reviewed against AVMA and small-animal emergency-medicine standards by our veterinary advisory board.
- [Medical disclaimer](https://dogemergency.org/medical-disclaimer/): DogEmergency.org is educational and is not a substitute for professional veterinary advice, diagnosis or treatment.
- [About DogEmergency.org](https://dogemergency.org/about/): DogEmergency.org helps dog owners across Asia recognise emergencies, reach veterinary care fast, and understand vet-guided recovery.

- Homepage: https://dogemergency.org/
